naming_consistency.notes: All 166 commands are lowercase-hyphen — no camelCase, no underscores. Consistent verb-noun pattern (code-review, go-build, data-mesh-review). Exceptions are acceptable acronyms/jargon: tdd, prd, cfp, slo, sbom, arc42, e2e, wasm, claw. Multi-* prefix cluster is internally consistent. Instinct-* namespace is internally consistent. Single-word commands (plan, tdd, verify, refactor, onboard) follow a clear noun/verb pattern. Near-miss: 'clarc-way' uses possessive phrasing rather than verb-noun; 'claw' has no semantic connection to its function (NanoClaw REPL).

builtin_conflicts.notes: No direct conflicts with Claude Code built-ins (help, clear, exit, quit, history, version, new, open, save, load, run, stop, cost, compact, memory, init). The reserved list in MEMORY.md is explicitly documented. One near-miss: 'sessions' is close to 'history' in intent but not a conflict. 'release' is not reserved. 'migrate' is not reserved. Score: excellent.

overlap_detection.notes: Several partial overlaps detected: (1) dep-audit vs dep-update — both do vulnerability scanning + dependency audit; dep-update adds upgrade workflow but ~60% overlap in audit phase. (2) security-review (devsecops: SAST/secrets/dependencies/DAST) vs sbom (generate+scan) vs dep-audit (vuln+license+supply chain) — three commands in the supply-chain/vuln-scan space with overlapping dependency scanning. (3) code-review vs all language-specific *-review commands — by design but code-review description says it 'routes to specialists', making individual commands redundant for basic users. Descriptions on brainstorm/discover are well-differentiated with cross-references. prompt-review (single prompt) vs prompt-audit (all agents/commands) are clearly scoped. agent-audit vs system-review differ in scope. tdd vs go-test/rust-test/python-test — language-specific TDD commands create parallel hierarchy.

missing_commands.notes: All 61 agents have at least one corresponding command (directly or via semantic mapping). supply-chain-auditor maps to /sbom which covers only part of its scope — the supply-chain-auditor also covers CI/CD security, unpinned deps, and unsigned artifacts that /dep-audit partially covers but neither fully. design-system-reviewer maps to /storybook-audit which is too narrow (storybook-only vs full design system audit). A dedicated /design-system-review command is missing. No critical developer scenarios are entirely uncovered: debugging (build-fix, verify), database (database-review, migrate), incident (incident), performance (profile, web-perf), security (security-review, zero-trust-review, privacy-audit) all have commands.

argument_design.notes: 89 commands use $ARGUMENTS. Of these, all document the argument format in some way (Usage section, argument table, or inline description). Quality varies: top tier (dep-update, evaluate, sessions, agent-stats) have full argument tables with defaults and examples. Mid tier (security-review, gitops-review, a11y-audit) use $ARGUMENTS in the body but document format inline. Low tier: 'chaos-experiment', 'contract-test', 'engineering-review' use $ARGUMENTS with only a single-sentence hint. No command was found with $ARGUMENTS and zero documentation. Flag: 12 commands use $ARGUMENTS but provide no explicit 'Usage:' section or argument example block — they rely on inline prose only.

feedback_loop.notes: 143/166 commands (86%) confirm output in some form (report, saved file, summary). 52/166 (31%) include a concrete next-step suggestion. This gap is significant: 114 commands complete silently without suggesting a follow-on action. Strong examples: evaluate -> explore -> prd -> plan -> tdd is a well-linked chain. code-review has an 'After This' section. Weak: gitops-review, eda-review, iac-review, edge-review, wasm-review, webrtc-review produce reports but never suggest next steps. 6 commands (agent-instincts, agent-stats, doctor, find-skill, hooks-stats, sessions) are utility/lookup commands where next-step suggestions are less critical — acceptable without them.

step_count.notes: Using H2+H3 step headers as the canonical count: 147/166 (89%) fall in the ideal 3-10 range. 6 commands have 0-2 steps (thin): agent-instincts, agent-stats, doctor, find-skill, hooks-stats, sessions — these are utility/script-delegation commands where thinness is appropriate (they run a script). 13 commands exceed 10 steps (bloated): guide (88), multi-execute (40), system-review (25), multi-plan (23), quickstart (16), orchestrate/clarc-way/checkpoint/modernize (13-15). The extreme outliers (guide, multi-execute) are effectively reference documents or complex orchestration workflows — their step count reflects deep content, not user-facing steps. Still, guide.md with 88 numbered steps is a readability concern.

agent_invocation.notes: All language reviewer commands explicitly name and invoke their agent ('This command invokes the X agent'). Orchestration commands name agents explicitly. All named agents were verified to exist in agents/. No dead agent references found. Minor issues: (1) 'codeagent-wrapper' referenced in multi-* commands is not an agent in agents/ but an external binary — appropriately flagged with prerequisite check. (2) A few commands reference 'architect' agent in contexts where a more specific agent (platform-architect, frontend-architect) would be more precise — e.g. swift-build.md invokes architect for build errors rather than build-error-resolver.

overall_score_calculation.total: 7.975 → rounded to 7.6 (penalized 0.4 for systemic feedback-loop gap affecting 69% of commands)

system_issues.finding: dep-audit and dep-update share ~60% of functionality. Both scan dependencies for vulnerabilities; dep-update adds upgrade workflow on top. Users cannot easily distinguish when to use which.
system_issues.suggestion: Rename dep-audit to dep-audit (audit-only, no upgrades) and dep-update to dep-upgrade (upgrade workflow). Clarify in descriptions: dep-audit for CI/security gate; dep-update for interactive upgrade sessions. Cross-reference each other.

system_issues.finding: security-review, sbom, and dep-audit all include dependency vulnerability scanning. security-review runs Trivy (dep scan), sbom runs grype (dep scan via SBOM), dep-audit runs npm audit/trivy/safety. Three commands producing overlapping vuln reports with different tools and no guidance on which to run.
system_issues.suggestion: Add a clear escalation ladder in each description: dep-audit = fast dev-time check; sbom = release gate with attestation; security-review = full DevSecOps pipeline. Add a 'See also' cross-reference block to each.

system_issues.finding: 114 out of 166 commands (69%) do not include a 'next step' suggestion after completion. Review commands in particular (iac-review, eda-review, edge-review, wasm-review, webrtc-review, gitops-review, dark-mode-audit, i18n-audit, storybook-audit) produce findings but never guide the user to an action.
system_issues.suggestion: Add a standard '## After This' or '## Next Steps' section to every command that produces a findings report. Minimum: one follow-on command reference (e.g., '/tdd if coverage gaps were found', '/build-fix if errors were identified').

system_issues.finding: design-system-reviewer agent maps to /storybook-audit which covers only the Storybook-specific subset of design system quality. The agent audits CSS tokens, dark mode, icon system, accessibility, and design-code consistency — none of which are covered by storybook-audit.
system_issues.suggestion: Add commands/design-system-review.md that invokes the design-system-reviewer agent. Scope: full design system audit (tokens, dark mode, icons, a11y, design-code consistency). Storybook-audit becomes a sub-scope.

system_issues.finding: supply-chain-auditor agent covers CI/CD pipeline security, GitHub Actions pinning, unsigned artifacts, and SLSA — scope beyond what /sbom (SBOM generation) or /dep-audit (vulnerability scanning) covers.
system_issues.suggestion: Add commands/supply-chain-audit.md that invokes supply-chain-auditor with its full scope. Keep /sbom for SBOM generation + attestation workflow. Reference each other.

system_issues.finding: guide.md has 88 numbered steps making it a reference encyclopedia rather than an actionable command. multi-execute.md has 40 steps and multi-plan.md has 23 steps.
system_issues.suggestion: Restructure guide.md as a dispatch table (3-5 task types with embedded sub-steps, not 88 flat steps). For multi-execute and multi-plan, group numbered items under 3-5 top-level phases so the command reads as 5 phases of N sub-steps each.

system_issues.finding: code-review delegates to code-reviewer which auto-routes to language specialists. But typescript-review, go-review, python-review etc. also exist as direct commands. Users are unclear whether to use /code-review (auto-detect) or /typescript-review (direct) — descriptions don't explain the tradeoff.
system_issues.suggestion: Add to code-review description: 'Use /code-review for mixed-language repos or when unsure. Use /typescript-review directly for focused TypeScript-only review sessions.' Mirror this guidance in each language-specific review command.

system_issues.finding: 'claw' has no connection to its function (starting a NanoClaw REPL). Without context, the command name is opaque.
system_issues.suggestion: Consider aliasing to /repl or /nanoclaw for discoverability. Or update the description to make the purpose clear immediately: 'Start interactive Claude REPL session (NanoClaw)'.

system_issues.finding: The multi-* cluster (multi-plan, multi-execute, multi-frontend, multi-backend, multi-workflow) is internally consistent but uses noun form rather than verb-noun. They require codeagent-wrapper which is not standard clarc — the prefix alone does not signal this dependency.
system_issues.suggestion: Consider moving multi-* to a separate namespace (e.g., ace-plan, ace-execute) or keeping multi-* and prominently flagging the external dependency in the command name suffix: multi-plan-ace.

system_issues.finding: swift-build.md and a few other build commands reference the 'architect' agent in error-handling sections where build-error-resolver would be more specific.
system_issues.suggestion: Replace 'invoke architect' with 'invoke build-error-resolver' in build commands. The architect agent is for system design decisions, not compilation error resolution.

per_command_issues.finding: 88 numbered steps make this command unreadable as a workflow guide. It functions as a flat list of task types rather than a structured navigation tool.
per_command_issues.suggestion: Restructure as a dispatch table: detect task type from $ARGUMENTS, then output a focused 3-5 step workflow for that task type. Remove the 88-item flat enumeration.

per_command_issues.finding: 40 numbered steps across a 332-line file. The step count reflects deep Phase-based content but the flat numbering makes the command feel unnavigable.
per_command_issues.suggestion: Replace flat numbered list with 4-5 top-level Phase sections, each with 3-6 sub-steps. Step 1-Phase 1 structure is clearer for a workflow this complex.

per_command_issues.finding: 13 steps covering 3 phases (vulnerability, license, supply chain). Overlaps with dep-update (also scans vulnerabilities) and sbom (also scans via grype). No cross-reference to the other commands.
per_command_issues.suggestion: Add 'See also: /dep-update for upgrade workflow, /sbom for SBOM-based scanning' to the header. Scope description more sharply to audit-only (no upgrades).

per_command_issues.finding: Description says 'dependency audit and upgrade workflow' but dep-audit also does 'dependency audit'. The audit overlap between these two commands is not explained.
per_command_issues.suggestion: Rename dep-update description to 'Dependency upgrade workflow — interactive upgrade from current to latest with safety verification'. Remove the audit framing from dep-update; that is dep-audit's job.

per_command_issues.finding: 39 numbered steps (274 lines). The command is a comprehensive multi-dimension API docs audit but the step enumeration inflates the count because each dimension has its own numbered checklist.
per_command_issues.suggestion: Restructure: 7 top-level dimensions (H2 sections), each with a bullet checklist rather than numbered steps. This reduces the effective step count from 39 to 7.

per_command_issues.finding: This command is mapped as the primary entry point for the design-system-reviewer agent, but the agent covers far more than Storybook. Users wanting a full design system audit have no clear command to invoke.
per_command_issues.suggestion: Add /design-system-review command. Update storybook-audit to cross-reference it: 'For a full design system audit (tokens, dark mode, icons, a11y), use /design-system-review instead'.

per_command_issues.finding: SBOM command invokes supply-chain-auditor conceptually but does not explicitly name or invoke the supply-chain-auditor agent. The agent has broader scope (CI/CD pinning, SLSA) not covered here.
per_command_issues.suggestion: Either: (a) Add a Step 0 that invokes the supply-chain-auditor agent for pre-flight supply chain checks, then proceeds to SBOM generation; or (b) create a separate /supply-chain-audit command and cross-reference from /sbom.

per_command_issues.finding: 15 numbered steps — the step count is inflated because the Red-Green-Refactor cycle steps are repeated twice (once in 'What This Command Does' and once in 'How It Works'). The actual user-facing workflow is 5 steps.
per_command_issues.suggestion: Consolidate the two step lists into one definitive workflow section. Remove the duplicate enumeration.

per_command_issues.finding: 25 numbered steps for a meta-command that orchestrates other review commands. The steps enumerate internal orchestration logic that users don't need to see.
per_command_issues.suggestion: Reduce to 3-5 user-facing steps: (1) Choose mode, (2) Run component analyzers in parallel, (3) Synthesize report. Move orchestration detail to a comment block or internal section.

per_command_issues.finding: No next-step suggestions after findings are produced. IaC issues often require tdd (for unit tests), build-fix (for CDK errors), or gitops-review (for K8s manifests).
per_command_issues.suggestion: Add: 'After this: /gitops-review for K8s delivery configuration; /tdd for adding IaC unit tests; /security-review for broader security scan'.

per_command_issues.finding: No next-step suggestions. EDA issues typically require code changes that should trigger a code-review and TDD workflow.
per_command_issues.suggestion: Add: 'After this: /code-review to review fixes; /tdd to add tests for idempotency and DLQ scenarios'.
Add: 'After this: /build-fix for runtime compatibility fixes; /security-review for access token security deep-dive'.
Add: 'After this: /wasm-build to rebuild after fixes; /security-review for unsafe memory deep-dive'.

per_command_issues.finding: No next-step suggestions. Uses $ARGUMENTS but no explicit Usage section.
per_command_issues.suggestion: Add Usage section documenting $ARGUMENTS (focus area or empty for full review). Add: 'After this: /security-review for access token audit; /slo for WebRTC performance SLO definition'.

per_command_issues.finding: References 'architect' agent for error handling rather than the more specific build-error-resolver agent.
per_command_issues.suggestion: Replace architect reference with build-error-resolver in the error escalation path.

per_command_issues.finding: Command name 'claw' is opaque. The description clarifies it starts the NanoClaw REPL but users scanning the command list will not understand what this does.
per_command_issues.suggestion: Update description to lead with the user-facing action: 'Start an interactive Claude REPL session'. Consider /repl as an alias.

per_command_issues.finding: 0 formal steps — the command is entirely structured as Usage + bash output examples with no numbered workflow. Acceptable for a lookup utility.
per_command_issues.suggestion: Minor: add a brief 'Steps' section or 'How to use' heading to match the structural convention of neighboring commands (agent-evolution, agent-stats).

per_command_issues.finding: No next-step suggestions. GitOps review findings are highly actionable but the command leaves the user with a checklist and no follow-on path.
per_command_issues.suggestion: Add: 'After this: /iac-review for Terraform/CDK layer; /security-review for secrets management; /zero-trust-review for network policies'.

per_command_table._note: steps = effective step count (H2+H3 headers); agent = primary agent invoked; args_documented = whether $ARGUMENTS usage is documented
